content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
March 24,
1993
Beverly Whitcomb
Deputy Executive Officer
PL 99-660 Planning Council
1600 Ninth Street,
Room 100
Sacramento,
CA 95814.
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-93-086
Dear Ms.
Whitcomb:
This is in response to your request for advice regarding the
California Mental Health Planning Council
(CMHPC)
under the
conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(“the
Act”.) 1
QUESTION
You have asked if
CMHPC must adopt a conflict of interest
code.
CONCLUSION
CMHPC must adopt a conflict of interest code because its
members make or participate in the making of governmental
decisions.
FACTS
CMHPC was created by Chapter 1374,
Statutes of 1992.
That
statute provides that
CNHPC shall have the powers and authority
necessary to carry out the duties imposed upon it,
including but
not limited to:
(c)
To review program performance in delivering mental
health services by annually reviewing performance
outcome data as follows:
1
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000,
.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Advice File No.
A—93 —086
Page
2
(1)
To review and approve the performance outcome
measures.
(2)
To review the performance of mental health programs
based on performance outcome data and other reports from
the State Department of Mental Health and other sources.
(3)
To report findings and recommendations on programs’
performance annually to the Legislature, the State
Department of Mental Health,
and the local boards.
(4)
To identify successful programs for recommendation
and for consideration of replication in other areas.
As
data and technology are available, identify programs
experiencing difficulties.
(d)
When appropriate,
make a finding pursuant to Section
5655 that a county’s performance is failing in a
substantive manner.
The State Department of Mental
Health shall investigate and review the finding,
and
report the action taken to the Legislature.
***
(g)
To make recommendations to the State Department of
Mental Health on the award of grants to county programs
to reward and stimulate innovation in providing mental
health services.
***
(lit)
To suggest rules, regulations,
and standards for the
administration of this division.
***
(0)
To employ administrative, technical,
and other personnel
necessary for the performance of its powers and duties,
subject to the approval of the Department of Finance.
(p)
(1)
To appoint an executive officer,
who shall have
those powers delegated to him or her by the State
Planning Council,
and who shall perform the duties
assigned him or her by the State Planning Council in
accordance with this chapter.
Section 48 of the enabling legislation sunsets the council’s
duties on January 1,
1995.
However,
cleanup legislation being
drafted proposes to extend the sunset to January 1,
1996.
ANALYSIS
Government Code Section 87300 requires every agency to adopt
and promulgate a conflict of interest code.
A conflict of
interest code is a document which specifically enumerates each of
the positions within the agency which involve the making or
participation in the making of decisions which may foreseeably
have a material effect on any financial interest.
For each such
enumerated position, the code must set out the specific types of
investments, business positions, interests in real property,
and
sources of income which are reportable.
Reportable interests are
those which may foreseeably be affected materially by any decision
made or participated in by the designated employee by virtue of
his or her position.
(Section 87302).


Advice File No.
A— 93—086
Page
3
Commission Regulation 18700 defines what it means to make or
participate in the making of government decisions.
Section
18700(b)
provides that a public official makes a governmental
decision when he or she, acting within the authority of his or
office:
(1)
Votes on a matter;
(2)
Appoints a person;
(3)
obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of
action;
(4)
Enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his
or her agency;... (Emphasis added.)
The enabling legislation empowers
CMHPC to employ personnel
necessary for the performance of its duties and to appoint an
executive officer.
That authority means that members of
CMHPC
have the ability to make governmental decisions as defined in
18700(b).
Additionally, Section 18700(c)
provides that a public
official participates in the making of governmental decisions
when, acting within the authority of his or her position,
he or
she:
(1)
Negotiates, without significant substantive review, with
a governmental entity or private person regarding the
decision; or
(2)
Advises or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker,
either directly or without significant intervening
substantive review,
by:
(A)
Conducting research or making any investigation
which requires the exercise of judgment on the part of
the official or designated employee and the purpose of
which is to influence the decision; or
(B)
Preparing or presenting any report, analysis or
opinion, orally or in writing, which requires the
exercise of judgment on the part of the official or
designated employee and the purpose of which is to
influence the decision.
Members of
CMHPC will make recommendations to the State
Department of Mental Health about the awarding of grants to county
programs to reward and stimulate innovation in providing mental
health services.
They will also review and approve the
performance of mental health programs and report findings and make
recommendations which require the exercise of judgment.
Thus,
members of
CMHPC will be participating in the making of


Advice File No
A—93—086
Page
4
governmental decisions,
as defined in 18700(c),
and are subject to
the disclosure and disqualification provisions of the Act.
Government Code Section 87303 requires a new agency to submit
a proposed conflict of interest code within six months of its
coming into existence.
We are enclosing information which should help you in putting
together your code.
If you have questions regarding this process,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—5662.
Sincerely,
Jeff Marschner
General CounsQ
By:
Wa
e P.
Imberi
Pofltical Reform Consultant

QUESTION: You have asked if
CMHPC must adopt a conflict of interest
code.

CONCLUSION: CMHPC must adopt a conflict of interest code because its
members make or participate in the making of governmental
decisions.
